hook: You already know how to solve many differential equations using algebraic methods, but some of the most important equations in physics — like those governing heat flow, quantum mechanics, and signal processing — simply don't yield to those techniques. The gap is that real-world equations often have variable coefficients that make closed-form solutions impossible. What if, instead of a single formula, the solution itself could be an infinite series?
concept: The unifying idea is that we can express solutions to difficult differential equations as power series, turning a calculus problem into an algebra problem by matching coefficients term by term — formally known as the method of series solutions (including the Frobenius method for singular points).

topic_roadmap.description: You will refresh your fluency with power series — convergence, radius of convergence, and term-by-term differentiation — so you have the algebraic toolkit needed to construct series solutions confidently.
You will learn to substitute a power series $y = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n x^n$ into a differential equation and derive a recurrence relation that determines every coefficient of the solution.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Series Solutions Near Regular Singular Points (Frobenius Method)
topic_roadmap.description: Master the Frobenius method by assuming a solution of the form $y = x^r \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n x^n$ to handle equations whose coefficients become singular at a point, using the indicial equation to find valid values of $r$.

Apply the Frobenius method to Bessel's equation $x^2 y'' + x y' + (x^2 - \nu^2)y = 0$ and discover the Bessel functions $J_\nu(x)$, which appear throughout physics and engineering in problems with cylindrical symmetry.
Apply series methods to Legendre's equation and recognize when the series terminates to produce the elegant Legendre polynomials $P_n(x)$, fundamental to problems with spherical symmetry in physics.
